db_end_state.title: Adding Voice Capabilities to AI Agents

sections.motivation: Text-only agents often struggle to capture the subtle nuances of tone and emotional intent that are inherent in spoken interactions. My experiences building a voice-first scheduling assistant highlighted several critical gaps where text-based models fall short. By incorporating voice, we can introduce a level of naturalness that significantly improves user trust and overall engagement. As we move toward a world of multimodal interaction, it is becoming increasingly essential for agents to process audio natively to keep pace with user expectations.
sections.ideas: There are several exciting directions for future exploration, such as using emotional prosody to make agent responses sound more empathetic or utilizing it as a signal for the agent's own confusion. Implementing "interruptibility" is another priority, where streaming partial transcripts allows a user to stop an agent mid-sentence naturally. Additionally, I am looking into voice biometrics for speaker-specific personalization and on-device processing to boost both privacy and speed. Finally, building robust fallback mechanisms that transition to text when audio quality drops will be essential for maintaining a reliable user experience.
sections.process: The process began by selecting low-latency TTS and STT providers. I implemented a streaming architecture and fine-tuned the LLM to manage conversational fillers. After wiring the STT frontend to the planner and integrating TTS output, I optimized the feedback loop for seamless turn-taking. Finally, I fine-tuned the model on noisy call audio and shipped it behind a feature flag.
sections.takeaways: Ultimately, low latency remains the most critical factor for maintaining the illusion of a natural, real-time conversation. It is important to recognize that voice is not just an interface but a distinct modality that fundamentally changes how prompts should be structured. Establishing continuous feedback loops is essential for identifying edge cases, especially in noisy environments where audio quality can vary. By successfully adding voice capabilities, we can significantly expand the accessibility and overall utility of AI agents for a wider range of users.
